reasoning: The question asks whether the Carnegie Global Protest Tracker will report a protest in South Korea with over 10,000 participants starting after the launch of the question (likely Jan 2025) and before July 1, 2025. Given that massive protests following Yoon Suk-yeol's martial law declaration began in December 2024, Carnegie's methodology will likely group these under a single protest "episode" with a December 2024 start date, thus failing the "start date after the launch of this question" criterion. A YES resolution would therefore likely depend on a *new*, distinct protest movement (e.g., large-scale KCTU labor rallies around May Day, or post-election unrest if early elections are held) emerging and being significant enough for Carnegie to track as a separate episode. Furthermore, there is a risk of a delayed update to the Global Protest Tracker, which may only report new 2025 episodes later in the year. These compounding factors—the risk of lumping under a 2024 start date and the strict tracker relevance criteria for new distinct massive protests—suggest a probability lower than simple baseline expectations for large Korean protests. I estimate the probability at 38%.
